title: Game Design with Stable Diffusion Forge UI & Photoshop
description:
In this tutorial, I'll show you how I design graphics for a Match-3 game called JunglePix using Stable Diffusion Forge UI and Photoshop. Learn how to create game elements like logos, loading bars, and backgrounds with these tools. Whether you're just starting out or already familiar with digital art, you'll find useful tips for using AI and Photoshop in your designs.

🔹 What You'll Learn:

- How to make a game logo in Photoshop.
- Setting up and using AI models like Juggernaut XL in Stable Diffusion Forge UI.
- Editing and finalizing game graphics in Photoshop.

🔹 Why Watch This Video?

- Get practical tips on using AI for game design.
- Learn simple Photoshop techniques for your projects.
- Find out how to handle common design challenges.

#GameDesign #PhotoshopTutorial #stablediffusion 

Chapters:
00:00 Game Design Intro
00:14 Logo Design  + ControlNet Settings
04:57 Loading Bar Design
06:13 Game Background Design
09:26 Grid Design
10:29 Button Design
11:56 Game Items and Icons
13:15
transcript: Today I will show you how I use stable diffusion and Photoshop to create the design for a match  three fictional game named JunglePix, where players need to match three or more similar objects  to score points and clear them from the board. I will start first with a logo, but probably I should  have designed it at the end after the game design so I can get it to match better with the game.  I am using Photoshop with a black brush and symmetry activated to draw on a white background  you can use any font for text and in stable diffusion I will get a style that matches the prompt.  I am using stable diffusion for UI installed locally on windows for the model  I will use Juggernaut XL which you can find on the civet AI website. I will use the latest version  number 10 the one with the Roman numeral x but other versions work okay too.  I still sometimes use version 9. You can download this model or any other model you like and  place it in the web UI folder then in the models folder and finally in the stable diffusion folder.  As you can see I already have both version 9 and 10 here. I will also use control net so you  need a candy model that you can get from this page. The one I use is the Koya control light XL  candy model. It has a download button and you can download it and put it in the web UI folder  then in the models folder and specifically in the control net folder. You can put all the control  net models you downloaded here. If your stable diffusion was open you can refresh it from here and  choose it from the list. You also have a refresh for the control net model. If you click on  candy it should add it to the model list. For the sampler I am using DPM plus plus 2M  carus with 30 sampling steps and 1024 pixels. Enable control net it should have that enable  check mark when it's enabled. Drop your sketch here then click on candy for the control type.  It should automatically put candy in the pre-processor. For the model use the one I just showed you  the rest of the settings can work on default but you can play around with sliders for control  weight and for control mode. You can choose other options depending on the image and prompt you  are using. For the prompt I mentioned keywords like logo and the text you want to appear. On the  first try I got something like this. I will use a fixed seed so I can play around with the prompt  and see what changes. I will use one of the saved art styles you can find them in my other video  with 260 art styles. I will use the 3D game asset style and generate again. I didn't like that it  didn't put a white background. It added some mask in the background too. So I will select that part  in the prompt and add more weight to it so it knows that part. It's more important. Press control  and the up arrow a few times. Now when I generate I get what I wanted. Once you are happy you can use  the high resolution fix to make it larger. You can either use a fixed seed and activate it from here.  I use the DATX2 upscaler with a denoise strength of 0.3 and upscale by 2. If it's inactive it will  still use those settings but you can click on this button with the star. That will upscale the  current generation and you will get a bigger version very similar to your original image depending  on the denoise strength you have set. Now you can open the logo and Photoshop. I like to use the  remove tool to fix the imperfections. Things that look like they should not be there. I will not  spend too much time on this since it's not a real project just for fun but you get the idea.  Then since it's on a white background you can use all kinds of tools to cut it out from the  background. The object selection tool and select object work okay or you can drag around the subject  to make a selection. Then if you apply a mask you can mask out that background. You can then  add a color background behind to check how good the cutout is. I missed this little hole during  recording and fixed it later. Then you can apply that mask and trim it so your document has the  same size as your image. Now it can be saved as a PNG and you can have a nice big logo that could  work for a game. By the way if you have no idea what to design you can ask chat GPT. Look at what  it suggests to create for logos, menus, backgrounds and so on. It can be quite useful. Back to Photoshop  I wanted to create a loading bar so I quickly added some round rectangles to get the shape of  the loading bar. On the left I wanted something like a Mayan sculpture. I didn't know exactly what to  draw so I just did some shapes there that could look like a side view statue or something.  Just enough information so AI can get the general shape and create something interesting from it.  Back to stable diffusion I will enable control net and upload the sketch I made.  I'll enable canny and make sure it's the right model. Then for the prompt I'll describe what I want  to appear. I will hit generate and get a nice bar. I will use the same art style with the 3D  game asset so it's more consistent and I got this version and now I can upscale it to get a bigger  version. Back in Photoshop I remove the background again. For the full loading bar I need some color to  show it's full so I was thinking to paint in green on those feathers from the wings. That is for  the loading bar. You can use the empty loading bar and then a mask to reveal the full bar.  I placed the logo and loading bar and I started sketching a landscape that I wanted behind. I  wanted a torch, some walls and some exotic plants with a Mayan jungle look. Now you still have to  make the sketch recognizable. My sketch was too simple and AI didn't see the waterfall in the  right too well so it added some walls instead. Since there are so many things in the scene it's  hard to get it all so I decided to add some colors so AI can identify better what is there. I painted  the torch in orange, the waterfall in blue and some greens for the plants. It's still a simple sketch.  Of course I can let AI generate how it wants things but I wanted to be guided by me to take my ideas  so it's more unique. This time since it has colors I will use the image to image tab. I will upload the  image and for the image size I will use a similar ratio to my sketch and for denoise strength I am  using 0.75. I will select the same 3D game asset art style from the styles. The result is completely  different because I didn't enable control net and the denoise is too big so I will keep the denoise  but this time enable control net with cany just like before and now we get an error, a non-type error  epic fail. It worked before and now it doesn't. Why? Well it must be a bug with the forge but  the solution in this case is to make sure the width and height are divisible by 64. In this case  720 divided by 64 is 11.25 so we can take the closest number which is either 11 or 12  and multiply it by 64. We get a number that will work and we can use that for the size.  Now when we generate we get something nice based on the ugly sketch I made. Of course since the  sketch is so rough you can get a lot of versions. You can either change the prompt, denoise strength  or generate a few versions until you get something you like. In this case I have a nice waterfall  and torch. Now what I like to do sometimes is to drag the result back to image to image then  I will try a lower denoise value and disable the control net. Now I can use the exact ratio I want  since it works when the control net is disabled. My video card can handle it so I will go with  full HD. I hit generate and I get a similar version in full HD and you can play around with  denoise strength and generate a few versions until you get something that works for you.  I think I will stop at this version. I will probably do some in-painting in some parts of the  image and the final result is this one for the background. I am using a software called DP  Animation Maker to animate parts of the image like the torch, the waterfall and some lava.  I also need a grid where the gems will stay on the main play window so I did something quick  from shapes making sure the thin lines have contrast. The first time I had the lines in dark gray  and they didn't show up well. I will use the same image to image process as before with the same  control net settings and a denoise strength of 0.72. The result was too black and white  so I added more words in the prompts especially golden hour to give it a warm color.  Now to upscale it I need to send it to the extras tab using that triangular icon. Here you can  choose an upscaler and the resize value. I will use two and the result is this grid. I can use  Photoshop to cut it out and test it on the background to see how it looks. Later I did some tests  with actual gems on it so I added a dark rectangle so the gems stand out better.  For the buttons I created two round rectangles with contrasting text so it's easier for  control net to detect the contours. I placed all the buttons on the same image to maintain a  consistent style and this time I'm using image to image so it can pick up a hint of the colors I want.  I will use a prompt generated with chat GPT for it and same game art style and I upload the buttons  image. With a denoise strength of 0.72 I hit generate. The result is too similar to my image. I  need it to be more 3D so I increased the denoise strength. Now it has more effects on the buttons  but it messed up the text so I will enable control net with canny so AI can see the contour of that  text and now the result is how I expected some nice buttons that look like they were crafted by  Mayans long time ago. In Photoshop you can cut it out from the background using the object selection  tool however in this case some of the white blends with the button edge so a better solution would  have been to add a round rectangle and clip that image inside it so it would have a clean edge.  The menu screen is starting to come alive now it's time to work on the you know the game items.  In Photoshop I created three shapes a square a triangle with rounded corners and a hexagon all  in different colors then in stable diffusion again with a long prompt from chat GPT and with that  image with the shapes uploaded in the image to image tab using the same art style with a denoise  of 0.75 and control net and canny enabled. I hit generate and got my first gems you can then  adjust the prompt and try to see what different variations you get the cool thing is that it's all  in the same style and fits the shape you gave if you increase the denoise strength you can get even  more variation but what I like is that you can change the prompt and you can get another set of icons  change the prompt again and get another one and so on until you get enough items for your game.  So the secret is to have a set of shapes in your image then use canny to get the contour of that.  If you have a hint of color you can influence the outcome but the prompt is also very important  as is the denoise strength. To have more control you can sketch out each icon for example next  I will do the cursor icon so in Photoshop I will do a quick sketch of how I want the cursor to look  I want it to be like a stone spear or something then since I didn't add any color I will just use  text to image with control net to to generate it. I got a mouse instead because I didn't add the  sketch on the control net so I am adding that and generating again I will hit generate until I get  a version that could work for me then in Photoshop I remove the background and then I test it on the  menu screen to see how it would look. That's all for today I hope you enjoyed the video leave a  like if you found something useful. Thank you for watching and have a great day.
